data.title: Marvell: Marvelous Return After Seven Months, Here Is My Strategy Going Forward (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) in the middle of September. With the stock around $69, I saw it as a “Buy,” and I initiated a half position, followed by another quarter at $77.4.

My investment thesis was (and is) underpinned by several key elements, starting with a good diversification around the hot data center segment, but also carrier and network infrastructure:

  • Compute: XPU (custom AI accelerators); CPO (co-packaged optics).
  • Opticals: DSP (digital signal processors); pluggable optical modules.
  • Network switches.
  • Storage: storage controllers and accelerators; DPU (data processing units).

Beyond the constantly increasing data center TAM, Marvell also came with a plan to increase their market share from about 13% in calendar 2024 to about 20% in calendar 2028. Then, I saw a narrow economic moat, with multi-year partnerships in XPU, leadership in DSP, and cross-sell opportunities from their integrated platform.

The stock is today at ~$164.3, with an impressive performance of ~130% in these seven months from my average cost (or ~138% from my previous article), partly due to new wins during this period, but mostly because the market finally recognized their value. I will present below my updated view about their business, fair value, and position.

Business Highlights

For FY'26 (ended Jan'26), Marvell reported great results for both reporting segments:

Let's start with the “Communications and other” segment. This segment, based especially on carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking, experienced this strong growth rate due to a normal strong recovery after significant declines in previous years. However, the expected forward growth rate for these more mature markets is about 10%.

For their “Data Center” segment, there are more notable things to mention:

  • Acquisition of Celestial AI, with its Photonic Fabric optical interconnect technology, which places optical connectivity directly at the chip-to-chip or chip-to-memory level, moving beyond traditional pluggable transceivers, especially useful for scale-up architectures. They expect a $1B annualized run rate from Celestial by Q4'29 (ending Jan'29).
  • Acquisition of Xconn, challenging Broadcom's detached leadership in Data Center switches. The company expects ~$100M revenue from Xconn for fiscal 2028 (ending Jan'28).
  • New possible AI chip partnership with Alphabet (GOOGL) for a memory processing unit intended to work alongside Google's TPU and for a new TPU specifically engineered for AI inference workloads.
  • $2B investment from Nvidia (NVDA) and a strategic partnership to connect Marvell to the Nvidia AI factory.
  • Acquisition of Polariton Technologies, with their plasmonic-enhanced photonic integrated circuits allowing for ultra-high-bandwidth at lower power than traditional silicon photonics.

Finally, one more note about their XPU business. In December, Marvell was downgraded by Benchmark from “Buy” to “Hold,” following their “high degree of conviction that the company has lost both Amazon's Trainium 3 and 4 designs,” causing the stock to slump from ~$98 to ~$89. A few days later, CEO Matthew Murphy refuted this report, and that was followed by some insider purchases at ~$78 at the end of September. The stock, after a short rise, continued its decline to ~$82 by mid-December.

Today, four months later, it's at $164.3. Not only did they not lose Amazon (remember, they have a 5-year agreement) or Microsoft, but they also won Google. The important thing to notice is that all these hyperscalers are not looking for exclusive deals but for multiple design partners, if possible. A second thing to notice is that this management seems neither stupid nor misleading.

From a geographical point of view, there is still a concerning dependence on China plus Taiwan:

However, this is not indicative of the company's end customers, with a substantial majority of the product shipments to China for “non-China-based customers that have factories or contract manufacturing operations located within China and whose products are subsequently shipped out of China.”

Fair Value

The company expects overall revenue in fiscal 2027 to grow more than 30% YoY, approaching $11B, meaningfully higher than their prior estimates. “Data Center” revenue growth is seen at 40% YoY, and for “Communications and other,” they expect 10% growth.

For fiscal 2028, with their custom business to at least double YoY and contributions from Celestial AI and Xconn, they expect Data Center revenue to grow close to 50% YoY. I think that management already had in mind the contributions from their recent Nvidia and Google partnerships. For their more mature Communications end market, they expect only low single-digit percentage revenue growth.

For calendar 2028 (fiscal 2029), their target from September was to reach 20% market share from a $94B Data Center TAM. I found that a bit optimistic in my previous article, and I modeled it only for a slightly optimistic scenario, but after these new wins, I am more confident, and I will model 19% of $94B for my base-case scenario.

For calendar 2029, I model 20% Data Center revenue growth, with a declining growth to low teens for XPUs and switches, offset by a stronger growth for opticals, especially with these new acquisitions. Then, I model gradually declining revenue growth from 15% in calendar 2030 to 4% in 2035.

For “Communications & other,” I model a more depressed 3% growth for fiscal 2028 following 10% in fiscal 2027 due to more cyclical mobile and auto markets, then 5% growth for fiscal 2029-2031, in line with their “single-digit percentage,” and then slightly declining to 3% growth.

Switching to profitability, I model gross margin expanding from ~51% in fiscal 2026 to ~54% in fiscal 2031, with higher scale and a mix dominated by next-generation products, offset by lower-margin custom chips in the short term.

I model R&D growth of 20% in fiscal 2027, significantly higher than for the last two years, with so many custom chips in design, followed by a gradual decline to 7% growth in fiscal 2031 (half of revenue growth). For SG&A, I model a peak growth of 20% in fiscal 2028, especially for promoting the new optical technologies like Photonic Fabric, followed by a decline to 5% in fiscal 2031 (the same half of revenue growth for S&M, offset by flat G&A).

This gives us ~35% EBIT margin in fiscal 2031. For fiscal 2032-2036, I model only slightly increasing EBIT margin from 37.5% to 40%, due to lower revenue growth and close to maximum scale. Their peer, Broadcom (AVGO), already has close to a 42% EBIT margin, but they also have a significant software business segment, so I keep modeling lower terminal margins for Marvell.

D&A is made of two parts. For Amortization of Intangibles, I add ~$200M for the next year brought by the new acquisitions, then I model ~10% decline per year, in line with the last two years. For the rest, I just model a constant 3% of revenue, following 3.5% CapEx.

With an estimated 16% tax rate, lower than usual due to high R&D credits, my 10-year estimates are (for more clarity, I am working with calendar years, for example, year ending December 2026 instead of January 2027):

The final assumptions for my DCF (discounted cash flow) model are:

  • 3% growth in perpetuity (close to GDP growth), pondered by 11x terminal EV/EBITDA.
  • 10.4% WACC (weighted average cost of capital), coming from 10.65% cost of equity (~95% weight) and 5% after-tax cost of debt (~5% weight). For cost of equity, I was starting from ~11% for a no-moat hardware company, but now I am starting from 11.15% due to increased country risk, then I subtract 0.5% for their narrow moat, 0.15% for their healthy financial position, and then I add back 0.15% for their China-Taiwan dependency. This is also close to Aswath Damodaran's 10.72% for the Semiconductor industry.

Resulting Fair Value is $132, about 20% potential downside from the current price ($164.3). With a high uncertainty given by high-growth estimates for at least five years, I rate Marvell as a “Hold.”

Strategy

Like mentioned in the introduction, I initiated an about 0.75x position in September for an average cost of ~$72. I generally target a full position for a narrow moat in the “Buy” zone, but not in a single trade. The stock started to grow, and my 0.75x position soon reached 1x due to price appreciation.

Then, I generally trim about half if/when the stock is around my Fair Value estimate (because my position is significantly above a full position and because it's only a “Hold” now), and I did that with Marvell for an average of ~$131, as mentioned through some comments on my previous article. With even more price appreciation, my position size is now close to 0.9x.

In a third step, when the stock is above my Fair Value estimate, but I don't want to sell my shares, I sometimes sell a slightly OTM (Out-of-The-Money) covered Call or an ATM (At-The-Money) Call Spread, to collect some extra premium while still leaving some room for price appreciation. I generally do that with the stock at half the distance between my Fair Value estimate and my Sell threshold. In this case, my Sell threshold is ~55% above my Fair Value, corresponding to a high uncertainty, offset by high growth. That gives somewhere between $165 and $170 for potentially selling a covered Call or Call spread.

Finally, as mentioned, I intend to fully close my position if it crosses my Sell threshold, meaning about 55% above my Fair Value estimate, or above ~$204, considering today's Fair Value.

On the downside, I will replenish my position if the combination of Undervaluation (potential upside to my Fair Value estimate) + Forward revenue growth + Free cash flow yield is greater than my uncertainty threshold. With probably the same 55% uncertainty threshold, I will likely replenish my position below ~$109, but only after double-checking my Fair Value estimate.

These thresholds can also be influenced by some short-term signals gathered from the options market, short ratio, technical analysis, share buybacks, or insider trading.

Risks

I already mentioned in my article this high uncertainty coming from high-growth estimates for at least five years.

For example, let's model only 15% Data Center market share for calendar year 2028 (remember, they had 13% in 2024, and they are targeting 20% in 2028; I modeled 19% in my base-case scenario), perhaps due to some overestimates for Celestial AI, Xconn, or for their Nvidia partnership, or who knows, perhaps due to a failure in one of their numerous XPU pursuits.

Even if we adjust R&D and S&M down to keep that ratio of half the revenue growth, the EBIT margin in calendar 2030 would be ~31.8%, due to this lower growth. Therefore, we also need to adjust down the EBIT margin for calendar years 2031 (let's say 34%)–2035 (let's say 36%).

In this case, Fair Value estimate is $98, significantly below my base-case estimate ($132) and way below today's price ($164.3).

And this is only due to one main input change (calendar 2028 revenue). I didn't change anything for subsequent growth (because those growth rates are more moderate), WACC (because I already see it relatively high today, influenced by high interest rates and geopolitical instability), or growth in perpetuity (because I can hardly see such a diversified semiconductor company unable to adapt to match GDP growth).

We can apply one more change: a 19% tax rate from 2029 (triggered by another US majority), and Fair Value would drop a bit more, to ~$96.

I sometimes average my base-case Fair Value estimate with my slightly pessimistic one, which gives us $114 in this case. Then I compare this value to the one given by the uncertainty threshold above ($109 in this case) as an alternative method to establish my Buy point.

Takeaway

Marvell's investment thesis is driven by Data Center growth, XPU partnerships (like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google), strategic acquisitions for the optical interconnects (Celestial AI, Xconn, and Polariton), and a new key partnership with Nvidia.

Management guides to 33%-34% revenue growth in FY'27, followed by another ~36% growth in FY'28, led by 40–50% data center segment expansion, new custom chip wins, and integration of Celestial AI.

My base-case DCF-derived Fair Value estimate is $132 (about 20% below the current price of $164.3), considering 19% market share of $94B data center TAM for FY'29 (around their 20% target). In a slightly pessimistic scenario, with only 15% market share and downward margin adjustments, Fair Value is $96.

I initiated about a 0.75x position at ~$72 in September, then I trimmed about half at ~$131, around fair value, due to price appreciation. I might sell covered Calls or Call spreads near $165–$170 for extra income, and I plan to fully exit above ~$204. On the downside, I plan to replenish my position below ~$109, considering today's Fair Value.

data.title: Certara: An Interesting Situation To Keep Track Off
data.description:
Shares of Certara (CERT) have hit the news wires after the company announced the sale of one of its subsidiaries last week. The deal is set to create a balance sheet with a net cash position, allowing Certara to focus on its core operations, while anticipating AI challenges and opportunities.

Investors hardly reacted to this sizable sale. I like the trend of valuations coming down, yet amidst flattish sales seen here (unlike growth in the past), investors are naturally a bit cautious here.

I am cautious, but intrigued as well, making me curious to keep track of the stock but not yet willing to pull the trigger.

Other interesting corporate events, including M&A stories, are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

The Sale Of The Regulatory Writing & Medical Writing Business

Certara has reached a deal with Veristar to sell its regulatory writing & medical writing business in a deal valued at up to $135 million. The business employs 220 workers and generates some $50 million in sales in 2025, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $17 million, which is ahead of unallocated corporate overhead.

Revenues were down 8% in 2025 as the company focused on its core business and commitment to the acceleration of AI-integrated modeling in drug development. The deal is valued at about 2.7 times sales and some 8 times EBITDA, again ahead of corporate overhead cost allocation.

Shares hardly reacted to the deal, trading at $6 and change with little additional volatility observed around the announcements.

Modeling Pro Forma Implications

In February, Certara reported its 2025 results, with full-year reported revenues up 9% to $419 million and constant currency sales reported up 8%. Growth was driven by an 18% increase in software sales to $183 million, with services revenues reported at $235 million, up a mere 3% from the year before. So far so good, as fourth-quarter sales rose by just 3% to $103 million, as the growth slowdown did not instill much confidence.

Bookings rose by 8% to $482 million, with the book-to-bill ratio reported at 1.15 times sales, adding to the backlog, which was not quantified. The company moved from a tiny GAAP operating loss in 2024 to an operating profit of $21 million, which, in between interest expenses and taxes, resulted in a GAAP loss of about a million.

Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.44 per share, or about $71 million in dollar terms, which is actually down a penny from the year before. However, this, among others, excludes a $33 million pre-tax stock-based compensation expense. If I adjust for these, earnings are seen around $46 million, closer to $0.30 per share.

Net debt is reported at $104 million, comprised of a larger gross cash and debt position, with net interest expenses for the year reported at $14 million. That could, of course, come down a great deal with this deal, although some earnings power will be foregone as well.

What About Growth?

With 160 million shares trading at $6 and change, the market value comes in around a billion, for a $1.1 billion enterprise valuation. This values all of Certara at 2.6 times sales and about 8 times EBITDA of $135 million here.

This makes for relatively similar valuation multiples, all while the divested assets are seeing revenue declines, and moreover, proceeds can be used to forfeit expensive debt and high-interest expenses.

A Flattish Outlook

Given the deceleration in the business in the fourth quarter, it is not surprising to see that the 2026 outlook did not feel that strong. Revenues are seen between flat and positive 4%, with EBITDA margins of 30-32%, down from a 32% number in 2025. All that should result in a pretty flattish adjusted earnings between $0.44-$0.48 per share, of course all ahead of the divestment here.

Given all this, I see the pro forma business being unleveraged, becoming a bit smaller, with realistic earnings seen around $0.30 per share. This largely results in a 20 times forward multiple for an unleveraged business with much slower growth here, or not any growth at all.

What Now?

Outside the divestment, further adjustments are taking place at the company, with CEO Jon Resnick taking the leadership role on the first day of the year.

Frankly, Certara has been a decent growth story over the past decade, having nearly tripled sales over the past decade amidst a modest 20% dilution incurred meanwhile. Some dilution was incurred over that time, with about half a billion being spent on M&A in this period of time.

While all this looks pretty solid, the reality is that investors have been in for a rough ride. After trading around the $40s when the business went public late in 2020, the share price has gradually come down to current levels, the lowest price seen ever since.

The idea and mission of the company is to create a new development for drug development. With 88% of clinical trials failing, many failures are driven by a lack of clinical efficacy, with small savings here adding up to big bucks as hundreds of billions are spent across the industry here. To that end, Certara has created an AI biosimulation model to simulate how drugs behave in the body, this being the core of its offerings.

The company seems to have become a lot cheaper recently, amidst continued growth and a falling share price, as organic growth is seen as flat. Following the larger divestment, the balance sheet is unleveraged, including a small net cash position. With shares trading around 20 times realistic earnings here, they are not necessarily dirt cheap. Of course, investors fear an AI overhang, although management and clients (reportedly) do not fear AI disintermediation, but clearly the market does.

For now, I am taking a curious stance, but at the same time, I am hesitant. Valuations here are very low in terms of sales multiples, although those earnings multiples (realistic earnings) are not necessarily dirt-cheap. This makes me curious given the valuation implosion, also given a net cash position maintained, historical growth delivered on, and realistic earnings reported. A lack of growth and AI concerns raise the bar to get involved. However, I like having met Certara here, but I am not yet willing to get involved here.

data.title: Cloudflare: Developer Platform Driving Stronger Growth
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Cloudflare (NET), we identified a company with a clear cost and scale advantage in the CDN market, driven by strong ISP partnerships and a highly accessible pricing model. We believed this positioned it to steadily gain market share while benefiting from long-term tailwinds like rising cybersecurity demand and increasing global data usage.

Since then, the company has performed exceptionally, with its growth outperforming our expectations at 29.85% in 2025 compared to our forecast of 22.37%, and management also guided growth to remain steady between 28% and 29% for FY2026. Its stock price has risen and reached past our previous price target based on our P/S valuation of the company. Given the company still has negative net margins and low FCF margins, we evaluate the company again to update our P/S valuation to determine whether the company could have further upside.

Stronger Revenue Growth

We first analyze the company's revenue performance. Its revenue grew by 30% in 2025, higher than our previous forecast of 22.37%, which we had derived based on our methodology of applying a calculated competitive score factor of 1.25x, driven by factors such as its attractive pricing, ISP partnerships, and broad product offerings, alongside the CDN market's forecast CAGR of 17.9%. This implies the company's revenue growth performance is stronger than our expectations, and to analyze its growth trend more specifically, we compiled its number of customers disclosed (end of the year) and calculated its ARPU.

From the table, Cloudflare's revenue growth has moderated from 2019, when it was growing at an average of 50% per year through 2022, to an average of 30.5% between 2023 and 2025. It has been steady, as 2025's growth is slightly higher than the previous year, and management guided between 28% and 29% growth in FY2026 as well. One interesting observation is that growth in the recent 2 years has mainly been driven by customer base expansion rather than ARPU growth, which in 2025 even turned negative. Customer additions have been rising in the past years, adding 94,752 customers in 2025, the most over the period, compared to 47,923 customers in 2024 and an average of 26k between 2020 and 2023.

Looking into the strong customer growth, the main reason stated by management in its earnings call for the increase was upgrades “from the free tier to small paid accounts, particularly for our developer platform products.” This refers to Cloudflare's Developer Platform, described as a platform that “provides a serverless execution environment that allows you to create entirely new applications or augment existing ones without configuring or maintaining infrastructure,” essentially allowing software and app developers to build and host solutions on Cloudflare rather than relying purely on hyperscale cloud providers like AWS or Azure (AZRH). It has a free tier (Workers Free), where developers can use basic features, and they can scale up to the paid version (Workers Paid) for $5 monthly with more advanced capabilities.

Management previously disclosed in Q4 2024 that it had crossed 3 million active developers on the platform, many of whom are still on the free tier, and according to SlashData, there are 47.2 million developers globally, indicating opportunities for continued customer expansion. The Developer Platform was launched in 2017, but we believe the recent growth could be attributed to the launch of Workers AI following the deployment of GPUs in more than 150 cities globally and its serverless SQL database in 2024, allowing developers to run AI models directly on Cloudflare's network and reduce dependence on traditional cloud providers.

In Q1 2025, management stated that Workers AI inference requests had increased by nearly 4,000% YoY. At the same time, we believe its ARPU growth, which turned negative in 2025, is also attributable to the rise of developer platform customers, as developer platform customers have about $60 yearly spending compared to the overall company ARPU of $6,521.

We forecast Cloudflare's revenue based on its customers and ARPU rather than our previous method of basing it on the market forecast CAGR and competitive factor score for a more aligned revenue growth with its performance. For the number of customers, as management's guidance for 2026 at 28.5% YoY midpoint growth is quite in line with the previous year, we modelled its growth similar to 2025, with a 40% growth, as we expect more customers to continue joining and upgrading to the Developer Platform.

Beyond 2027, we based its customer growth on its 3-year CAGR and tapered it down as it grows from a larger base. On the other hand, for ARPU growth, we modelled it to continue declining in 2026 due to the rise of Developer Platform customers, which currently have lower spending than larger enterprise customers. Beyond that, we based it on its 3-year average and expect it to gradually improve over time, supported by potential upselling opportunities as these customers scale up, such as increased storage, higher-priced tiers, or additional cybersecurity features.

P/S Multiple Analysis

In the next section, we examine the P/S multiple of the company and the broader Internet Services & Infrastructure subindustry of the key top players. In our previous analysis, we had derived a simple average P/S ratio of the top players of the subindustry of 15.44x. To determine a more appropriate multiple accounting for the high growth, we plotted the chart below of the P/S ratios and revenue CAGR (5-year).

The chart above shows the top 13 companies in the Internet Service and Infrastructure subindustry with a market cap above $2 billion, along with their 5-year revenue CAGR and average P/S ratios over the period. The plotted chart shows a clear positive trend, as companies with higher revenue growth have higher P/S multiples.

From the chart, Cloudflare's historical average P/S of 30.57x is actually above the trendline considerably, with a historical CAGR of 38.48%. We also compiled Cloudflare's P/S by year to analyze its trend. Its P/S ratio shows an interesting trend where it was the highest and much higher than the average in 2021 at 62.56x but was followed by a steep decline as its stock price declined by 66% amid the tech sector sell-off and also slightly lower growth in 2022 compared to 2021. Though its P/S ratio has been picking up over the years, despite its growth continuing to slow, the 2025 ratio is the highest it has been since 2022. This might indicate investors being more optimistic about Cloudflare's growth prospects following its developer platform developments and expansion targeting developers creating AI applications, as well as the rising customer growth.

For our valuation, we derive the multiple based on the subindustry trendline. From the trendline, our revenue forecast CAGR of 25.8% indicates a P/S multiple of 16.0x, which we believe reflects a more appropriate multiple for the company given our forecast CAGR being lower than its historical 5-year CAGR, despite the strong customer growth, as our forecasts gradually taper down and are weighed down by lower ARPU from the customer growth.

Stock-Based Compensation and Dilution Risk

For our P/S valuation, we examine the company's stock-based compensation as it accounts for a significant portion of its expenses and examine its shares outstanding.

The table shows Cloudflare's SBC, which has risen over the years and stabilized at around 20% of its total revenue in the past 4 years, showing its SBC growing fairly in line with its high revenue growth. This seems significant, as the SBC for the company includes share issuances for its employees as incentives, like RSUs and stock options, which would dilute the total shares, but the shares outstanding have only grown by single digits, a 5-year average of only 3.1%.

For 2026, management guided diluted shares to be 377 million, which is an 8.2% growth from 2025 (348.4 million) that includes RSUs and stock options. The company also has convertible notes, which, if exercised by the company to repay these loans with equity instead of cash, would further dilute the shares. The company has two convertible notes that mature in 2026 and 2030, which are 6.8 million and 8.075 million shares that could be diluted. Though management guidance stated that their guidance does not assume convertible note shares, indicating they will repay with cash rather than issue shares.

We overall expect Cloudflare's stock-based compensation to continue growing strongly as the company continues to grow rapidly, and its SBC has tended to follow its growth. This will likely lead to dilution and an increase in shares, which we account for in our forecast, using 2026 guidance of 377 million shares and growing it by its 5-year average of 3.1% to reach 425.2 million by 2030, which would reduce our yearly valuation but only tepidly. A potential upside could be the company initiating a share buyback program as its FCF strengthens over time as it grows and scales its margins, but until then, management has not announced or indicated any future buyback program; thus, we assume an increasing share count for our valuation for the time being.

Risk: Competition With Cloud Hyperscalers

One of the risks for Cloudflare's developer platform is the competition from traditional cloud hyperscalers, as its expansion with the developer platform offering a platform to host applications, along with the introduction of GPU-accelerated servers and SQL databases, puts it in competition with hyperscalers, which have larger and more established cloud ecosystems of cloud solutions. Another strength of hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Google, and Amazon (AMZN) is the direct integration of frontier AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google Gemini, which are among the top-ranked AI models in the world.

Valuation

For the valuation, we update our P/S valuation method for the company with our revised revenue forecasts and shares outstanding. Our revenue forecasts have a 5-year forward CAGR of 29.5%. Applying the multiple derived from our trendline analysis in the second point to our 2030 revenue forecast and prorating it back to 2026, we obtain an upside of 19.96%, rising to 47.76% by 2030, as its revenues grow despite P/S normalizing to the trendline average.

Verdict

All in all, we believe Cloudflare's revenue growth has exceeded our expectations, driven by strengthening customer growth attributable to the expansion of its Developer Platform targeting software developers building AI applications. While this has weighed on ARPU due to lower spending from developer platform customers compared to its traditional enterprise CDN customers, we see this as a near-term tradeoff that could present longer-term upsell opportunities as these customers scale. Based on our updated P/S valuation, derived from the subindustry trendline and factoring in share dilution from its high stock-based compensation, we still see positive upside for the company, supported by stronger revenue growth than previously expected, with a 5-year forward average of 29.5% compared to 26.5% previously. As a result, we raise our price target to $241.11 from $200.99 previously, reflecting improved growth visibility and continued expansion opportunities, and rate the company as a Buy.

data.title: TCAF: I Remain A Skeptic Owing To Mostly Disappointing Performance, Factor Mix
data.description:
I am of the opinion that it is unlikely that the T Rowe Price Capital Appreciation Equity ETF (TCAF) will outrace the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year. I also believe that the probability for it outperforming the S&P 500 ETF over the medium term is comparatively low too.

I previously discussed TCAF in December 2024, highlighting its decent but nonetheless mostly unimpressive performance, as well as factor nuances supportive only of a Hold rating. By and large, concerns remain nearly the same today, so I see no reason for turning bullish on the name. And it is my pleasure to elaborate on the key reasons why I still find TCAF unappealing below in the note.

Reason 1: TCAF Has No Meaningful Advantages Over IVV on the Factor Front

As we know from the fact sheet available on the ETF's website, the actively managed TCAF "seeks to provide long-term capital growth" and has a "focus on investing in a diversified portfolio with strong return potential and lower risk relative to the S&P 500 Index."

But are the exposures that its portfolio offers at the moment supportive of outperformance? Although they are certainly robust, I suppose that to outcompete the S&P 500 ETF, they should be even stronger, principally in terms of growth and GARP characteristics, which I believe are crucial to achieving alpha amid de-escalation.

More specifically, as of April 22, there were 91 equities in its portfolio, with the top ten accounting for 44.7% of the net assets. The majority of holdings are S&P 500 companies (86.9% weight, as per my calculations), plus the portfolio is very much about the trillion-dollar bellwethers (36.8% weight vs. 39.1% in IVV), so for the most part, exposures that TCAF is offering are not profoundly different from those of IVV. Nevertheless, upon a more attentive inspection, critical vulnerabilities still can be found.

Growth, Value, and GARP Characteristics

As illustrated by its weighted average forward earnings per share growth rate of close to 20%, TCAF has a sizable exposure to high achievers. With that being said, the WA forward EPS and EBITDA growth rates of its portfolio are nonetheless lower than those of IVV, and its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher is weaker to boot. I believe this will more likely translate into underperformance.

There is a nuance with the forward revenue growth rate, though. This figure is substantially higher than IVV's despite other growth rates being lower, which begs the question about what could have driven this anomaly. My answer here is that the sector mix and stock selection have contributed.

The issue is that TCAF is nearly 11% overweight in health care compared to the S&P 500 ETF. And two healthcare stocks that are responsible for such an impressive WA forward revenue growth rate are CG Oncology, Inc. (CGON) and Cytokinetics, Incorporated (CYTK).

Stock Weight in TCAF Revenue Growth Fwd
CGON 0.3% 399.5%
CYTK 1.1% 172.9%

Data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF

And even with these outliers' growth rates downsized to 100% to reduce their impact on the weighted average figure, the portfolio's WA revenue growth rate is at 18.23%, which is still stronger than IVV's. But, again, as I said above, TCAF is well behind the S&P 500 ETF when it comes to earnings and EBITDA growth, which I believe is of greater importance.

As to value and GARP metrics, alas, the picture is not completely clear.

  • The TCAF portfolio has a weaker adjusted earnings yield and a diminutive allocation to stocks with at least a B- Quant Valuation grade. At the same time, the portfolio appears cheaper on a P/S and EV/EBITDA basis.
  • Its portfolio has higher EV/EBITDA-to-Growth and PEG ratios compared to IVV. The PSG ratio is lower, though, owing to the health care exposures we discussed above.
Metric TCAF IVV
PEG Ratio (the WA TTM P/E divided by the forward EPS growth rate) 1.59 1.23
P/S-to-Growth Ratio 0.45 0.57
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.15 1.12

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Quality

In terms of quality metrics, the TCAF portfolio is behind that of IVV. What I find the most disappointing is that its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher is only 82.9%. I would prefer seeing it at least in the high 80s and, ideally, in the low 90s.

Low Volatility

Besides, TCAF has a slightly larger exposure to low beta (with that coefficient below 1) stocks (45.17% weight vs. 39% in IVV). This could be beneficial in a correction, and there is still a possibility that peace talks will fail, thus igniting a market-wide valuation reset. However, in a strong recovery, which is my base case, exposure to low volatility is more of a disadvantage, as it curbs the upside capture.

Reason 2: S&P 500 ETF Has Stronger Quant and SA Analysts' Ratings

What I also find worth mentioning is that the weighted average Quant and Seeking Alpha Analysts scores of the S&P 500 ETF portfolio are stronger than those of TCAF.

However, the TCAF portfolio is still sporting a stronger WA Wall St. score, mainly thanks to SPX Technologies, Inc. (SPXC), CG Oncology, Inc., and Vaxcyte, Inc. (PCVX), which IVV has no exposure to.

Stock Weight in TCAF Wall St. Score
SPXC 0.34% 4.75
CGON 0.25% 4.73
PCVX 0.43% 4.72

Data from the ETF and Seeking Alpha

Reason 3: TCAF's Unconvincing Performance

Since its inception in June 2023, TCAF has delivered an over 56% total return, which is a healthy result. However, it has nonetheless underperformed IVV by almost 12%.

TCAF has also underperformed the S&P 500 index since my December 2024 article.

Next, what annualized active return (with the S&P 500 ETF used as a benchmark) would I expect or demand from an ETF that has a 31 bps expense ratio (more than 10x larger than IVV's) and 29% turnover? I believe that at least a few bps is a good place to start. However, over July 2023–March 2026, TCAF's active return was -3.18%, which is, of course, quite far from desirable, especially considering its downside capture ratio was close to 99% while the upside capture was at only 86.79%.

However, there are positives too. For example, TCAF had a much lower standard deviation than IVV, plus its maximum drawdown was a little more comfortable. But we should not overlook a nuance here: IVV's maximum drawdown over the period was delivered in October 2023, and TCAF's was delivered in March 2026 (IVV's maximum drawdown was -5.77% that month). The quite obvious corollary here is that TCAF was less prepared for the ripple effects of the Iran war, and if the peace talks end to no avail and trigger a sell-off, it might suffer more than the IVV ETF.

Final Thoughts

I have been consistently neutral on TCAF for a few years, and I do not see any reason why I should turn bullish today. In my view, the ETF has more or less healthy but certainly not convincing returns, and its factor mix, while mostly decent, is still weaker than that of the S&P 500 ETF, mainly in terms of growth and quality metrics. Overall, I do not expect outperformance from this vehicle and thus maintain the Hold rating.

data.title: Nomura: IB Could Recover In Ebullient Q1, Macquarie Integration Costs Will Pass
data.description:
Nomura (NMR) is significantly indexed towards wealth and asset management as well as trading and IB businesses. As noted in previous coverage, the IB segment is indexed to lower visibility business lines, and overall dealmaking did take a dive in March, globally reflected in the results, but volatility has been quite good for the rest of the business. As for AM (asset management), there are one-offs that should start attenuating through the course of the year from the Macquarie acquisition that should increase segment margins, and wealth management is generally quite solid and is performing at leading margins, doing a good job of servicing customers amid volatility. With the markets recovering a bit as of the end of March, it's possible that the next quarter, Q1, will see a recovery in capital solutions activity with possibly a resumption of IPOs and issuances. On the other hand, lower volatility could give back some of the demand surge coming into some parts of wholesale but return demand for high-yield products, helping both fund sales in wealth and asset management as well as for yield products in wholesale. While wealth management and asset management are generally resilient either way, an appreciating market helps these business lines overall by growing the AUM and recurring revenue base. In terms of valuation, in typical fashion for Japanese equities despite global presence, Nomura is on the cheap side at 11x PE, cheaper than Raymond James (RJF), which has similar IB revenue exposure, Morgan Stanley (MS), and Piper Sandler (PIPR), which has similar sales and trading exposure though more IB. We like that the profits are momentarily depressed by one-offs and some of the structural demand benefits right now of IB in Japanese markets.

Latest Results

Asset Management

The asset management business acquired a portion of Macquarie's (MCQEF) asset management businesses. Consequently, revenues grew nicely on the consolidation, and flows were good into Japanese trusts and Japan active products. Therefore, despite market declines, AUM growth since December occurred. Note also the much more limited EBIT growth in the segment relative to revenue. This is driven entirely by acquisition expenses associated with the Macquarie acquisition. The majority of the costs are expected to be digested within a year, starting from the next quarter, where the next year should be a successful lap of this pressure.

Related to the Macquarie acquisition costs:

These costs will be incurred over the next 2 years, but the majority is expected to be recognized over the 1-year period starting from the fourth quarter

Hiroyuki Moriuchi, CFO NMR, Q3 Earnings Call

Also, there was quite a lot of growth in alternative asset AUM, around 400 billion JPY in the quarter before this one, which is driving a nice proportion, maybe around 10%, of the organic growth without considering Macquarie in AUM and the latest AUM stall on account of the war. That growth continued in Q4 quite unabated, up around 300 billion JPY; it was an important vector for the result of another sequential growth in AUM. It's a nice bump that shows the growing adoption of the asset class through Nomura's Asia-focused platform.

Wholesale Segment (Sales and Trading, IB)

The wholesale segment includes the IB division, including also Greentech. The IB business was driving most of the growth as of Q3. IPOs had been successful, particularly in Japan. DCM (debt capital management) also did well in Japan in light of changing yield curve dynamics and the drive for companies that are overcapitalised in that market to adopt more leverage to hit the TSE ROE targets. Advisory did well, particularly in Japan, again for similar reasons as overcapitalised companies invigorate corporate strategy with bigger acquisitions - but financing solutions drove those results.

Now, that has naturally shifted due to the fragility of the financing solutions business. These low-visibility businesses risk getting shelved in the event of market turmoil, and the sequential decline in favour of advisory in the mix was to be expected, but the parts moving in opposite directions slowed down overall IB growth to a single-digit pace. The only secular area here mentioned by the company is another Japan market quirk, which is that solutions activity was quite strongly associated with the unwinding of cross-shareholdings, another area that the TSE is trying to address to increase market PBRs.

Spread product sales were quite solid despite yield curve changes, but sidelined clients saw fixed income revenue take a hit, with equities and execution services demonstrating unsurprising pro-volatility growth in a quarter wracked by the Iran War.

Wealth Management

Wealth management is benefiting secularly from Japan-focused savings activities as savers deal with higher inflation and look to shore up their investments in international markets, as well as higher rates of activity. The transaction component of this business (the flow revenue) rose in Q4 on account of the greater volatility in roiled markets, accelerating since the previous quarter. Certain rate-based products fell in sales on account of yield curve change dynamics, and the base for recurring revenues was down, but the volatility drove results nicely.

It's a margin-leading business, with margins in the 40s as opposed to the 30s compared to peers. When asked about it in the call of the previous earnings:

Nomura's Wealth Management 100% sales are in-house. And because of that, partly because of that, it's easier to control cost.

Hiroyuki Moriuchi, CFO NMR, Q3 Earnings Call

Banking

Banking developed solidly in terms of loan growth, but the high growth in non-interest expenses associated with the deposit sweep setup hit results. This is targeted for full release this year. Therefore, despite the revenue growth, EBIT development was weaker.

Risks

While there are countercyclical levers in transaction volume-related revenues in wealth management and in the wholesale business, wealth management would not benefit from lower market valuations in terms of funds under management in an adverse market scenario. IB, which in NMR's case is very indexed to the lowest visibility activities of ECM and DCM, is likely to take a hit in an adverse market, such as that which may materialise in Q1 if the Iran War reignites following, for example, failed ceasefire and peace talks. Asset management, similar to wealth management, doesn't benefit if AUMs fall due to market depreciation. While volatility is supposed to be good for the wholesale business, that's not always the case - there could be a lot of waiting on the sidelines in product lines if NMR's customers want to wait and see what happens given the uncertainty of a geopolitically driven market. So it's not necessarily going to be the case that NMR is well positioned in an adverse market environment that has arisen in Q4 and may be coming in Q1.

On the other hand, if the low volatility environment persists, some surge in demand could retreat in transaction-based revenues, which isn't great for that part of the WM business as well as for wholesale's trading and sales business.

But overall it is quite a resilient business in our view due to the diversified financial services it offers.

Bottom Line

On valuation, at 11x PE, it's cheaper than RJF, which is 15x on a trailing basis, and PIPR, which is 22x. RJF has a similar IB exposure in revenues and also a banking division, though Nomura is much more indexed to trading. Morgan Stanley isn't such a bad comp in terms of mix either, and they're at 17x. They used to be on a similar playing field, though Nomura had some EMEA retreats about a decade ago; both indexed quite heavily towards trading-related businesses, but then also with a meaningful IB component as well.

Another approach is that IB is approximately 20% of Nomura's wholesale business, which in turn is around 34% of operating income (we assume banking and wholesale margins are the same, and also we are doing these shares with the Q4 data that reflects run rates best post-acquisition). AM and WM together are almost 64%, with the balance in banking. An AM pureplay around 12x seems fair to us in the current market. Moelis (MC) is an IB pure play and is at around 22x in forward PE. Taking Flow Traders (FLTLF) as perhaps a pure-play trading multiple at 10x could be done, although a larger-scale wholesale banking operation may be worth more than that. Nonetheless, blending those multiples from pure plays in a weighted average based on EBIT shares (12*0.28+22*0.06+12*0.64+10*0.02) with banking given a standard 10x PE (it's a very small share and barely impacts the calc), around 11.8x would be a fair multiple. Nomura is at 9.9x on a forward basis (with forecasts of a full year of the Macquarie-acquired contribution), so there's some gap there.

We think their business model is overall quite defensive in the various adverse and ebullient market scenarios that we have seen materialise so far in 2026. At a discounted multiple, and with current income depressed by integration expenses, particularly in the investment management business, we see potential for income growth. The indexation to Japanese markets should also be a positive, as corporate governance reform incentivizes strategic and secular M&A and leveraging activity. We like Nomura.

data.title: Peapack-Gladstone Financial's Strong Q1 Bodes Well For Remainder Of 2026
data.description:
Peapack-Gladstone Financial Corporation (PGC) reported its Q1 2026 financial results on April 22. Net income of $14.2 million translated into earnings per share of $0.80, $0.11 better than analysts were expecting on an adjusted basis. When compared to last year’s results from the first three months of the year, Q1 2026 was an improvement of 86.0% in EPS, signaling a trend of rapid profit growth that is expected to extend throughout this year and into 2027.

Based in Bedminster, New Jersey, Peapack-Gladstone is the holding company for Peapack Private Bank and Trust. The company has a history that dates back over 100 years, but the most recent decade for PGC has seen the company transform into a boutique private bank that specializes in wealth management, trust and fiduciary services, and advisory services for high-net-worth individuals. The stock struggled to find its way for several years post-Covid, as its price in late February of 2021 was about the same as it was at the beginning of 2026. However, this year has been a much different story, as investors in Peapack-Gladstone have enjoyed gains of over 41%.

Given the strong start to the year, it is fair to ask if there are more gains to be seen in 2026. After looking through the company’s most recent earnings report, I think the answer may be yes. The valuation for PGC is still compelling enough to warrant a buy rating, even with a few considerable risks in place, as I will explain in the analysis below.

Company Overview

Peapack Private Bank & Trust operates approximately 25 financial centers in a three-state area, with the majority located in its home state of New Jersey. Four are in the metro New York City area, and one is located just outside of Wilmington, Delaware. PGC has a market cap of $703.9 million with total assets of $7.7 billion. Its wealth management division had $13.1 billion in assets under management at the end of the most recent quarter.

As of March 31, 2026, PGC reported its gross loans were valued at $6.4 billion, an increase of 11.9% from Q1 2025. Since 2012, Peapack’s banking operations have experienced an impressive 14% compound average growth rate for its loan portfolio. Total deposits held by the company were worth $6.8 billion at the end of the most recent quarter, an 8.6% improvement year-over-year.

The largest share of the bank’s outstanding loans is commercial and industrial, comprising 43.8% of the total loan mix. Second in allocation are multifamily loans at 28.1%. The majority of those loans are connected to rent-regulated facilities, as shown in the graph above. Commercial real estate makes up only 14.1% of the gross loans from the bank, with a value just under $1 billion.

While deposits have not been growing quite as fast as the company's loan portfolio, good progress is being made in opening new non-interest-bearing accounts. Over the last 12 months, the amount held in these accounts has increased by roughly 30%. During that same time period, the bank's cost of interest-bearing deposits has dropped from 3.14% to 2.52%.

Dividend income is not a significant reason to own Peapack-Gladstone, as its stock yields only 0.50%. The company pays out $0.05 to shareholders on a quarterly basis. That amount has been the same since Q3 2009.

Analysis of Peapack-Gladstone Financial

There has been a notable shift in profitability for Peapack-Gladstone that can explain much of its stock’s recent performance. In FY 2023, the company reported $2.71 in earnings per share. The following year, that number had fallen to $1.85 as the company’s interest expenses were growing faster than interest income. However, in 2025, the company was able to post full-year earnings of $2.10, a 13.5% bounce-back improvement that was the result, in part, of a 34.8% increase in net interest income.

A closer look at the company’s recent quarterly performances shows the momentum that has been building into this year. The chart below shows the last four quarterly earnings for the company, including the most recent Q1 from this year. It also shows analysts’ projections for the remainder of 2026.

Earnings Per Share

The trend of increases is expected to continue through the end of this year and into the next. Analysts expect PGC to post EPS of $0.88 in Q2 2026 and at least $3.68 for FY 2026. If the company hits that full-year target, it will represent a 75.2% improvement from FY 2025. Expectations for FY 2027 are almost as optimistic as the company is projected to earn $4.69 per share.

There are several reasons for the company’s improved fortunes. A higher net interest margin is pushing up net interest income. NIM rose 18 basis points in the first three months of 2026 and 58 basis points from Q1 2025.

The company is seeing improvement in its performance ratios, although many of them are admittedly lower than where I usually like to see them in a small-cap regional bank. In the cage of PGC, it is the trends that started last year and continued through the first three months of this year that are noteworthy. Return on average assets was only 0.74% in Q1 2026, but that was a 31-basis point improvement from Q1 last year. Return on average equity stood at 8.51% in the first three months of this year, 353 basis points more than during the same period in 2025. The bank’s efficiency ratio has fallen from 76.82% in Q1 2025 to 67.21% in its most recent report. Continued improvement in ROA and ROE, along with further declines in the bank’s efficiency ratio, should support the higher earnings totals expected for later this year and into 2027.

PGC is also seeing the benefit of greater account balances in its wealth management accounts. Assets under management have grown an average of 10% over the last four years. Even with a volatile Q1, wealth management revenue is on pace to improve almost 5% in FY 2026, based on the results from the first three months of the year. Q1 fee revenue of $16.5 million was 6.9% ahead of what was recorded in Q1 from last year.

From a valuation standpoint, PGC is trading at a sizeable discount to the market when compared to other regional banks. At the end of Q1 2026, the stock had a tangible book value per share of $39.48, 12.5% higher than at the end of Q1 2025. As I write this analysis, one share of Peapack-Gladstone Financial costs $39.44, so the stock is basically trading at its tangible book value. It is this discount that allows me to have more comfort with the low ROA and ROE ratios mentioned earlier.

Risks to Consider

Asset quality is an issue to watch for Peapack-Gladstone in upcoming quarters. There are no single numbers on the slide below that are particularly troublesome, but as I stated with the company's performance ratios, often the trends are more important than the numbers themselves. The ratio of special mention loans to total loans rose 36 basis points from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026, with almost all the increase apparently tied to New York Rent Regulated Multifamily loans. The ratio of past due loans increased 31 basis points to 0.73%, with those same rent-regulated loans accounting for an even greater share. Nonperforming assets did tick downward in the first three months of this year, the third straight quarter of declines for this statistic. The ratio of nonperforming loans has also declined for three straight quarters, ending Q1 2026 at 0.92% after being as high as 1.97% in Q2 of last year.

Because wealth management is an important part of PGC’s operations, a pullback in the stock market would likely impact not only the total assets under management for the company but also the fees that they are able to generate from those assets. The US economy has proven to be remarkably resilient thus far in 2026, as long-term interest rates have risen and the conflict in the Middle East has created instability in energy prices. Should the Iran conflict linger into the summer, inflation could cut further into consumer spending and provide additional headwinds. The odds of a recession in the second half of this year seem to be fluctuating between 30% and 40%.

The Bottom Line

Without consideration of where the company has been over the last couple of years, the results posted by Peapack-Gladstone Financial in Q1 2026 are nothing to write home about. However, the multiple quarters of operational improvements and the impact they are having on the company’s bottom line are much more compelling. The deflated price-to-TBV ratio also makes the investment thesis here easier to support. The business climate in New York City and the pressures there being put on high-wealth individuals, a group that has become increasingly important to PGC’s success, are certainly worth following throughout this year, but I still believe the stock has the potential to continue its run from the first three and a half months of 2026.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: April 24, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished April 24, 2026, at 4.31%. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.78%, and the 30-year yield ended at 4.91%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September 2024 and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have been on the decline as of late, following a similar fashion to the FFR. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.23%.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

For a long-term view of weekly Treasury yields, also focusing on the 10-year, see our latest Treasury Yields in Perspective update.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT).
