data.title: IUSG: Concentrated Exposure To Growth Stocks
data.description:
The iShares Core S&P U.S. Growth ETF (IUSG) is among growth ETFs offering targeted allocation to growth stocks. As is usually the case, the fund has a strong exposure to mega caps and technology companies, which also translates into above-average profitability measures. This is a characteristic of the fund that is amplified now compared to my previous article covering the fund in early 2025. While earnings growth expectations in the overall market and particularly in big techs are likely to continue to support IUSG’s performance from here, its stock concentration profile and no significant advantage over growth peers suggest a more measured, though still overall optimistic view on the fund. With that in mind, the following sections will break down the fund’s core holdings and compare its sector allocation, valuation, and performance with the Russell 1000 and other growth ETFs.

ETF Description & Highlights

IUSG is designed to provide exposure to large- and mid-cap U.S. companies with above-average growth characteristics. The fund tracks the S&P 900 Growth Index, a subset of the S&P 900, which is the merger of the S&P 500 and S&P MidCap 400 indexes.

From this initial universe, the index provider assigns three factors for each company, based on the following measures: 3-year sales growth, 3-year earnings growth relative to price, and 12-month price percentage change. These inputs are combined into a composite growth score that is used to select the index constituents and weight them in the index. The weighting process is subject to constraints that limit individual positions to roughly 23% of the index, while also restricting the aggregate weight of all holdings above 4.8% to no more than 50% of the index. In addition, through a quarterly rebalance structure, the methodology maintains the portfolio aligned with current market conditions and avoids excessive concentration in a few stocks.

As of April 17, 2026, IUSG had $29.6 billion in assets under management, invested in 386 companies, with an average market capitalization of $770.2 billion. The allocation is heavily skewed toward the upper end of the market-cap spectrum, with mega caps making up 64.3% of total assets, followed by large caps at 24.5%, while mid caps account for only 8.1% and small caps 2.9%. This represents an increase of nearly 4% in mega-cap exposure compared to early 2025, in a shift that suggests a greater significance of larger companies when it comes to growth relative to the rest of the equity market.

IUSG’s tilt toward larger companies is also clear when compared to market-cap-weighted indexes like the Russell 1000. The fund has much greater participation in mega caps (64.3%) than the benchmark’s allocation of 43.4% of the index, which is admittedly high but not as much as in IUSG.

IUSG’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (14.13%), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) (9.05%), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (5.73%), Apple Inc. (AAPL) (5.72%), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) (5.56%), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (4.58%), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) (4.34%), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) (3.74%), Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) (2.51%), and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) (2.12%), which together represent 57% of the fund. This highlights the fund’s heavily concentrated profile in big tech, along with a few other large names from other segments, such as Berkshire Hathaway and Eli Lilly.

The table below includes a mix of large-cap growth funds, with the last two incorporating active stock selection, which will serve as the peer group for IUSG in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), the largest in the group, which tracks the CRSP US Large Cap Growth Index using a market-cap-weighted methodology. The next fund is the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), which follows a similar capitalization-weighted approach, selecting companies from the Russell 1000 based on growth characteristics. The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, also relying on market-cap weighting, while the Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF (MGK) follows an index that focuses primarily on the largest companies in the U.S. equity market. The JPMorgan Active Growth ETF (JGRO), on the other hand, incorporates fundamental research to build a portfolio of growth-oriented companies. Lastly, the Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF (FBCG) applies an active, bottom-up approach centered on high-growth names, resulting in a portfolio tilted towards mega- and large-cap companies, similar to other funds in the peer group.

From a sector allocation perspective, IUSG is heavily tilted toward technology (48.2%), which stands well above the rest of the portfolio. A second tier includes communication services (16.6%), consumer discretionary (9.4%), and financial services (9.0%), providing a mix of platform-driven and cyclical growth. Further down, industrials (7.4%) and healthcare (6.4%) are still relevant sectors, while the remaining allocation is spread across consumer staples (1.0%), real estate (0.8%), basic materials (0.6%), utilities (0.5%), and energy (0.2%), all representing only a marginal fraction of the fund. Of note, the fund’s heavy tilt toward two sectors, technology and communication services, has only intensified since early 2025, as the exposure to technology rose sharply by 8.7% and communication services edged higher by 2.7%, while most sectors declined, particularly consumer discretionary (-4.2%) and financial services (-2.8%).

As a result, IUSG now appears heavily overweight in technology (+14.5%) and communication services (+5.9%) to a lesser degree, while the fund is underweight in all other sectors, especially in consumer staples (-3.7%), financial services (-3.3%), and energy (-3.2%), highlighting a positioning away from more value-oriented segments.

If we break down IUSG’s core holdings across its largest sectors, we see that in technology, the allocation is quite concentrated at the top, even though it spans nearly 70 holdings overall. A meaningful portion of the exposure is in semiconductors, with large positions in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), followed by Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD). There’s also a sizable exposure to software, led by Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), with smaller holdings in Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD), and Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW). In summary, the fund’s heavy exposure to semiconductors still offers a solid upside tied to AI buildout, a demand that appears largely intact in the foreseeable future. On the other hand, while prospects in software are less certain, due to uncertainties about the potential impact of new tools enabled by AI in software makers’ business models, Microsoft, the fund’s core position in the industry, remains well-positioned to monetize cloud demand. Elsewhere, while the fund maintains marginal exposure to other industries within the sector, Apple Inc. (AAPL) remains a core holding, weighing in at around 5%, giving the fund exposure to iPhone demand, which is arguably a more mature segment, but still a high-margin business.

Moving to communication services, the exposure is even more concentrated. Despite holding almost 20 stocks, core holdings are by far Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), two high-margin businesses anchored in ad spend, while also investing heavily in AI infrastructure. Another position worth highlighting is Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), the streaming platform operator that continues to grow its subscriber base, with a business model that has been resilient to competition and less cyclical than initially feared.

Likewise, in consumer discretionary, although the fund owns nearly 50 companies, positions in Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) account for the bulk of the sector sleeve. Here, Amazon’s AWS and its online retail scale are clear growth drivers, while Tesla’s outlook is more mixed, with expectations of volume growth amid rising competition and pricing pressure. Elsewhere, the fund holds companies in the travel and leisure segment, such as Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG), Marriott International, Inc. (MAR), and Royal Caribbean Group (RCL), where healthy travel demand underscores a positive outlook for the industry despite potential higher fuel costs. Rounding out the sector exposure, holdings in auto retailers like O’Reilly Automotive, Inc. (ORLY) and AutoZone, Inc. (AZO), where demand is relatively steady and somewhat more defensive than cyclical areas, given that demand for auto parts remains consistent in tighter economic conditions, when consumers hold vehicles for longer. Thus, this is an area where we should not expect growth as high as in sectors like technology, for instance.

Looking beyond the top sectors, the portfolio leans into a mix of long-cycle industrial themes, big pharma, and high-margin payment names. In industrials, core holdings include RTX Corporation (RTX) and GE Aerospace (GE), where budgets remain supportive, particularly given ongoing geopolitical tensions, as well as Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) and Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR), which tend to benefit from secular demand for construction, electrification, and energy infrastructure. At the same time, Eli Lilly (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) offer a relatively diversified and stable source of income, while positions in Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA) remain poised to benefit from the continued shift toward digital payments, though growth may be subdued for a while due to softer consumer spending. Overall, the setup looks balanced and arguably well-positioned for a moderate growth environment.

IUSG’s allocation to technology is broadly consistent with its growth peers, such as VUG, IWF, and SCHG, all with positions in the mid-40% to mid-50% range. Similarly, the fund’s communication services exposure of around 16% is also close to most peers. On the other hand, IUSG has a lighter allocation to consumer discretionary, trailing peers like FBCG and IWF, while its exposure to financial services is by far the largest in the group, as growth ETFs clearly underweight this typical value sector. That said, despite these differences, IUSG’s sector correlations remain extremely high within the group, with all peers sitting in the 0.97-0.99 range.

Premium Valuation Relative To The Benchmark

At a 22.6x P/E, IUSG trades at a 12% premium to the Russell 1000’s 20.2x. This is largely explained by the fund’s outsized allocation to technology, given that the sector remains close to half of total assets. But that is not the only factor driving IUSG’s valuation higher. The fund’s stock selection in sectors such as communication services, healthcare, and industrials also plays a role, since the fund is overweight in several companies in these groups that trade at considerably higher multiples than the benchmark. Core examples include Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Netflix Inc. (NFLX) in communication services, Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG) in healthcare, and GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) and Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM) in industrials.

As expected, the fund clearly delivers on growth. Its historical earnings growth of 21.2% handily surpasses the Russell 1000’s 8.8%, while its sales growth of 13.3% is also well above the benchmark’s 7.4%. Again, the main driver is the fund’s heavy exposure to technology, along with holdings that have delivered stronger earnings and top-line growth. This pattern is also seen in most sectors, especially healthcare, industrials, and energy, where earnings have increased at least 10 percentage points faster than the Russell 1000.

IUSG’s profitability metrics are clearly ahead of the Russell 1000 as well, with an EBITDA margin of 41.3% that is about 6.7 percentage points above the Russell 1000’s 34.6%, while its ROE of 52.0% also outpaces the benchmark’s 41.7%. The biggest reason remains the technology and communication services sectors, where core holdings in high-margin names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) drive profitability up. Still, holdings in sectors like financial services, healthcare, and consumer staples are also higher than the benchmark. This suggests IUSG is not only a growth-oriented fund. It also exhibits a significant quality tilt, as the mega caps that are overweighted by the fund operate in high-margin businesses, such as big techs, as well as names like Eli Lilly (LLY) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA).

Overall, IUSG’s core strategy naturally pushes the fund toward a richer valuation profile, as companies with stronger growth and price outperformance tend to trade at a premium. The same goes for profitability metrics, as much of the earnings growth of the equity market continues to stem from high-margin big tech names. Of course, if market leadership broadens further from here, that may result in greater participation from other sectors in the fund, but as the time window for the price momentum in IUSG’s methodology is the trailing twelve months, it would take some time for a change in market leadership to be reflected in the fund’s stock picking. That said, assuming no big changes in the fund’s composition and given its business resilience in its core holdings, my view is that IUSG should remain a fund that exhibits not only above-average growth, but profitability as well. And this is a good thing, especially in a softening growth environment that we are likely to see over the coming months.

Due to the high sector correlation among peers, it is not surprising that the peer group as a whole trades at a premium relative to broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000. This also makes sense given the peer group’s heavy exposure to mega caps. That said, IUSG trades at nearly an 11% discount to the group, likely driven by the influence of one of the measures used in its methodology, the so-called “earnings growth relative to price”, which includes not only earnings growth, but also adjusts it for stock price. This results in an indicator that tends to score higher whne the stock price is relatively low and vice versa. Thus, as we can see, this measure appears to increase the weight of companies that trade at lower prices in the fund, compared to other peers that apply a straightforward growth methodology.

Performance In Line With The Peer Group

With strong annualized returns of 26.6% (3-year) and 13.7% (5-year), IUSG has handily outperformed the Russell 1000. These solid gains have been roughly in line with the peer group, especially SCHG, FBCG, and MGK, which also achieved strong performance, reflecting their heavy exposure to mega caps and the technology sector.

The fund’s sector mix has led to higher volatility, though. Its beta of 1.11 (3-year) and 1.14 (5-year) is higher than the Russell 1000, but a bright spot is that it has remained below the peer group average of 1.20-1.22, indicating a relatively more balanced risk profile within the category. This is also illustrated during the correction in 2022, when it held up better than all peers, despite the underperformance compared to the Russell 1000 in that period.

It is interesting to note that, by looking at IUSG’s capture ratios, we can see that they have been lower than all peers on both the upside and the downside, which reinforces the fund’s more contained price action compared to other growth funds, despite having a high sector-mix correlation within the group.

Source: Morningstar, consolidated by the author

Like most of its peers, IUSG’s average daily volume of $147 million is solid. Still, its bid-ask spread of 0.13% is wider than more liquid funds like IWF (0.01%) and VUG (0.02%). The fund’s turnover of 21% is moderate and sits below the peer group average, while its expense ratio of 0.04% remains one of the lowest in the group and well below peers with active strategies such as JGRO and FBCG. IUSG offers a 0.58% yield, slightly above the peer group, though this is not a significant differentiator for a growth fund. Tracking error is relatively contained within the group, with IUSG consistently posting lower figures than most peers. Finally, its Sharpe ratio of 1.11 (3-year) is the highest in the group, highlighting top risk-adjusted returns in the period, while the reading of 0.53 (5-year) stands modestly above the peer group, due to a combination of price gains in line with peers and below-average volatility.

Upside Drivers and Risk Considerations

IUSG’s upside remains anchored in AI capital spending, cloud demand, digital advertising resilience, and solid earnings delivery from the mega-cap category in general. With the market pointing to big techs and the technology sector among key contributors to earnings growth, there is a good reason to believe the fund remains well-positioned in the foreseeable future.

The concern is that this strength now comes with a higher bar. Expectations are arguably high for the fund’s core holdings, and their upside is largely dependent on this narrow group continuing to deliver strong growth. In my view, that does not undermine the fund’s long-term appeal, but it does make the near-term setup more nuanced, especially after broader equity indexes have hit new highs in recent days, despite potential macro headwinds such as the likelihood of an extended period of elevated energy prices and a mixed growth outlook.

Final Thoughts

IUSG’s investment methodology not only offers exposure to larger companies with a strong growth profile but also gives the fund a high profitability and quality tilt. In addition, valuation seems still reasonable compared to peers, though the fund trades at a premium relative to broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000. Based on a market backdrop that remains supportive for the equity market, as estimates for S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026 remain solid in the mid-teens, it is reasonable to expect IUSG and other growth peers to deliver double-digit returns over the coming twelve months. That said, while I am reluctant to give IUSG a buy rating for the time being, given the premium valuation relative to the Russell 1000 and no consistent outperformance relative to the peer group over longer time frames, my take is that IUSG makes sense as a core holding for investors seeking growth exposure and diversification in the category.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: Growth Continues To Impress, But Valuation Is Getting Lofty
data.description:
I'm providing an update for my coverage on Amprius Technologies (AMPX). The company reported Q4 2025 results and had some additional developments since my last article. Amprius is achieving strong revenue growth along with margin expansion. The margin gains are driving the company towards profitability. While I love the growth that Amprius is achieving, I still have valuation concerns.

The company background information can be read in my first Amprius article.

AMPX's battery technology received the Best of Innovation award at CES for its silicon anode lithium-ion batteries. Amprius was chosen over thousands of entrants. The deciding factor came down to AMPX's batteries providing 520 watt-hours per kilogram. That is 2x the energy density of conventional graphite-based lithium-ion cells. This high-energy density enables the batteries to perform much better in their key applications.

Growth Catalysts

Amprius finished 2025 with strength as the company grew revenue by 137% to $25.2 million, beating estimates by $2.33 million in Q4. This helped drive the 3x gain in revenue to $73 million for the full year. These strong top-line results demonstrate the demand for AMPX's high-density silicon anode lithium-ion batteries. The company's batteries have 2x the energy density, range, and flight time as compared to conventional graphite-based battery cells. This advantage helps drive the demand for the use of AMPX's batteries for drone usage.

Amprius has also been achieving strong margin increases. The gross margin increased from a negative (21%) in Q4 2024 up to a positive 23.7% in Q4 2025. The gross margin increased from a negative (76%) for the full year of 2024 up to a positive 11.3% in 2025.

The operating margin is still negative. However, the margin improvements that Amprius is making are getting the company closer to profitability. The operating margin did improve from a negative (184%) in 2024 to a negative (33%) in 2025. Amprius may get close to profitability in 2026 but will more likely achieve it in 2027. AMPX is expecting a net loss of less than $8 million, or $0.06 per share, in 2026.

Amprius gained a major customer for unmanned aerial vehicles [UAVs]. The company picked up Nokia Drone Networks as a customer. AMPX's batteries provide Nokia drones with sufficient burst power for takeoff with sustained energy for extended flights along with safe returns/landings. AMPX's technology enables drones to fly longer, to carry more, and to operate in challenging conditions. This helps customers to reduce downtime and increase safety, which increases the overall value.

Amprius is ahead of its efforts to become NDAA compliant. This involves meeting the U.S. National Defense Authorization Act requirements for batteries. The compliance requires batteries to be assembled by a non-foreign entity of concern [FEOC]. So, that means to meet the requirement, the batteries must be assembled in the United States or in an allied nation.

Also, the functional cell components must be sourced from a non-FEOC. Compliance with this should help Amprius to obtain new contracts and/or extensions of existing contracts from the U.S. government. AMPX has until January 2028 to achieve full compliance. AMPX has three South Korean contract manufacturing partners and added a U.S.-based partner with Nanotech in California. An example of a U.S.-based award was a recent increase of a Department of War Defense Innovation Unit award in July 2025, which was increased to $14.8 million. This award is for Amprius to accelerate production of NDAA-compliant SiCore pouch cells used in military unmanned autonomous systems.

AMPX secured a $21 million purchase order from a new electric mobility customer in China for its SiCore cylindrical cells. Amprius' cells are to be used in this customer's light electric vehicles, which include scooters, motorcycles, and 3-wheelers. AMPX's SiCore cells were chosen for their high-energy density and long cycle life.

Amprius obtains about 75% of its revenue from outside of the U.S. That is a result of the specific markets that AMPX serves. For example, AMPX cells are used in Ukraine for drones that fly above 20,000 feet while carrying devices such as radar, LiDAR, cameras, a gimbal, communications equipment, and a secure flight controller. That adds a lot of weight to the drone, which creates the need for batteries that can handle the load. That's where AMPX's high-energy-density cells serve that need. Also, about 75% of AMPX's revenue comes from the defense market for these UAVs.

The light EV market comprises approximately the other 25% of AMPX's revenue. This market tends to be concentrated in China and other parts of Asia. However, the company has been gathering momentum in Europe for high-powered motorcycles, power tools, and similar products.

So, this leaves growth opportunity in the United States. Amprius anticipates that the U.S. will play catch-up for its products. AMPX sees the space market as a potential growth opportunity, where standard lithium-ion batteries fall short of handling that market's needs (i.e., backup power for antennas & receivers).

AMPX sees growth potential for electric vertical takeoff and landing [eVTOL] vehicles. These eVTOLs demand the high-powered cells that Amprius provides to launch effectively. One positive aspect of this application for AMPX is that it has more replacement needs as compared to EVs or stationary storage.

Amprius is estimating 2026 revenue to be $125 million. For the longer term, AMPX is targeting to achieve revenue of $600 million by 2030 with 30% gross margins and 20% EBITDA margins. The $600 million target is based on a total addressable market [TAM] of $2 billion just for batteries for the UAV space.

Valuation

The market tends to award Amprius with a premium valuation. It is not clear whether or not that will be sustainable over the long term. AMPX is trading with a forward price/sales ratio of 23.6x. This is significantly higher than the sector median forward price/sales of 1.9x. This is much higher than the forward price/sales of 8.5x when I wrote my November 2025 article.

Amprius does have strong long-term growth potential. So, I understand the market's enthusiasm for AMPX. However, I can't be confident that the market will just keep bidding the valuation up higher from here. I think the current valuation level puts the stock at risk for a steep correction on any unexpected disappointing news. That could include missing analysts' quarterly estimates or some other negative news.

Technical Analysis

AMPX's daily chart shows a bearish divergence between the stock price and the RSI from mid-March 2026 to the present. This occurs when the price makes a higher high, while the RSI indicator makes a lower high. Bearish divergences like this typically result in pullbacks from what I have observed. We can see in the chart that the stock also had a bearish divergence from September to October 2024. This resulted in the stock dropping from about $16 to just below $8.

The RSI is technically still bullish as it remains above the 50 level. A move below 50 would confirm a bearish trend. The MACD is also still technically bullish, as the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line. If the blue line crosses below the red signal line while the histogram bars turn red, it would also confirm a new bearish trend.

AMPX's next earnings report, scheduled for May 7, 2026, for Q1 2026 results, is the next likely catalyst. Analysts are expecting Q1 revenue to be $25.72 million and normalized EPS of -$0.02. AMPX's positive revenue momentum should allow the company to meet/exceed these estimates. Any news of new contract awards could be another positive catalyst for the stock.

Risks for the Stock

Competition could be a risk for Amprius. The company competes with Solid Power (SLDP), which makes sulfide-based batteries for EVs. QuantumScape (QS) is another competitor that produces lithium-metal batteries for EVs. SES AI (SES) produces lithium-metal and lithium-ion batteries for EVs, robotics, drones, etc. These competitors could take market share away from Amprius if their technology were proven to be more effective or desirable. It is also possible that these competitors increase their market share in the drone market, where Amprius has been thriving.

Since Amprius is still unprofitable, the company may have a need to issue more stock to continue to fund operations. AMPX issued $71 million in common stock in 2025. This was 50% higher than the $47.9 million of common stock that was issued in 2024. These equity raises can dilute shareholders as more shares enter the market. A large equity raise has the potential to trigger a sell-off for the stock.

Amprius Technologies Long-Term Outlook

The revenue growth that Amprius has been achieving continues to impress me. This helped drive strong margin expansion over the past year. Continued strong revenue gains and margin expansion should get AMPX to reach profitability approximately in 2027.

My concern remains that the valuation is lofty. The market may continue to give Amprius a premium valuation for its above-average revenue growth. However, I'm not comfortable with the current high forward price/sales ratio. The technical setup also points to a near-term possible correction for the stock. As a result of this, I will maintain my rating at 'HOLD' for AMPX.
